schools.summary: Gill: this is the first song recorded in Scripture, sung unto the Lord for his glorious triumph over Pharaoh; the witnesses note Moses and the children of Israel singing this song to the LORD for his glorious triumph, the horse and rider thrown into the sea — the first song of Scripture, the song of redemption raised after the deliverance at the sea (the redeemed breaking forth in praise: 'I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously: the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea'; the occasion, that this is the first song recorded in Scripture, sung by the whole congregation after their salvation at the Red Sea; the matter of the praise, 'for he hath triumphed gloriously,' that God had won a glorious and complete victory over Pharaoh, the proud enemy; the figure of the triumph, 'the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea,' that the LORD with ease cast horse and rider together into the deep, the might of Egypt swallowed up; the type the commentators draw, of the song of the redeemed, 'the song of Moses... and of the Lamb,' sung by the church delivered from her enemies; that salvation calls forth the praise of the saved).

debates.question: What does the song of Moses at the sea prefigure?

sides.position: The song of the redeemed — 'the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb' (Revelation 15:3) — sung by the church delivered from her enemies; salvation calls forth the praise of the saved

themes: I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously
the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea
the first song of Scripture, the song of redemption after the deliverance at the sea
a type of the song of the redeemed, the song of Moses and of the Lamb

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD was the strength of Moses and Israel against their fears, inspiring them with courage, and their song, the matter of their praise; the witnesses note the singers owning the LORD as their strength and song and salvation, their God, to whom they would prepare a habitation and whom they would exalt — the heart of the song, the LORD himself the strength and joy and salvation of his people (the redeemed claiming God as their own: 'The LORD is my strength and song, and he is become my salvation: he is my God, and I will prepare him an habitation'; the threefold confession, 'my strength and song, and... my salvation,' that the LORD was their strength against fear, the matter of their song, and the author of their salvation, all in one; the personal owning, 'he is my God... my father's God,' that they claimed him as their own covenant God, the God of their fathers, theirs by inheritance; the resolves of love, 'I will prepare him an habitation,' build him a sanctuary, or some render glorify him, 'and I will exalt him,' that gratitude moved them to honour and exalt their Saviour; that this verse is taken up again in the Psalms and Isaiah, the abiding song of the redeemed).

themes: The LORD is my strength and song, and he is become my salvation
he is my God, and I will prepare him an habitation; my father's God, and I will exalt him
the LORD owned as strength, song, and salvation, the covenant God of the fathers
the abiding song of the redeemed, taken up again in the Psalms and Isaiah

schools.summary: Gill: 'a man,' which has respect to the future incarnation of Christ, for as yet he was not really man, though it was purposed, covenanted, and prophesied; he is a man of war, mighty to save; the witnesses note the song declaring the LORD a man of war, whose name is the LORD — the praise of God's might in battle, with a glance at the incarnate Christ the warrior (the LORD owned as the mighty conqueror: 'The LORD is a man of war: the LORD is his name'; the title, 'a man of war,' that the LORD is mighty in battle, who fights for his people and overthrows their enemies, as he had now done to Pharaoh; the Christological glance Gill draws, that 'a man' looks to the future incarnation of Christ, who, though not yet really man, was purposed and prophesied to be, the Lord Christ a man of war, mighty to save, who would war against and conquer all his and his people's foes; the name, 'the LORD is his name,' that Jehovah, the self-existent and almighty God, is he who thus fights, his very name the pledge of victory; that God's people need not fear, having such a warrior on their side).

outliers: 'a man of war' (Gill): a Christological glance at the future incarnation of Christ, the Lord Christ a man of war, mighty to save
themes: The LORD is a man of war
the LORD is his name
the LORD owned as a man of war, mighty in battle for his people
a glance at the incarnate Christ the warrior, mighty to save, his name the pledge of victory

schools.summary: Gill: this was done by the Angel of the Lord, who was Jehovah himself, our Immanuel and man of war, an emblem of the destruction of Christ's enemies; the witnesses note the song telling how Pharaoh's chariots, host, and chosen captains were cast into the sea and drowned — the celebration of the overthrow of the whole might of Egypt (the song recounting the ruin of the enemy: 'Pharaoh's chariots and his host hath he cast into the sea: his chosen captains also are drowned in the Red sea'; the completeness, 'Pharaoh's chariots and his host hath he cast into the sea,' that the whole pursuing army, chariots and men, was hurled into the deep; the greatness of the overthrow, 'his chosen captains also are drowned,' that even the best and choicest of Egypt's warriors perished, none escaping; who wrought it, that it was done by the Angel of the Lord, who was Jehovah himself, our Immanuel, the same man of war; the type the commentators draw, of the destruction of all Christ's enemies, the proud powers that pursue his people swallowed up; that God can in a moment overthrow the greatest might that rises against his people).

themes: Pharaoh's chariots and his host hath he cast into the sea
his chosen captains also are drowned in the Red sea
the whole might of Egypt cast into the sea and drowned, none escaping
a type of the destruction of all Christ's enemies who pursue his people

schools.summary: Gill: the depths of the sea covered Pharaoh and his host, so as to be seen no more, and in like manner shall mystical Babylon be destroyed; the witnesses note the song saying the depths covered the Egyptians, who sank to the bottom as a stone — the picture of the utter and irrecoverable ruin of the enemy (the sea swallowing them wholly: 'The depths have covered them: they sank into the bottom as a stone'; the completeness of the ruin, 'The depths have covered them,' that the deep waters closed over Pharaoh and his host, hiding them from sight, seen no more; the figure of their sinking, 'they sank into the bottom as a stone,' that they went down swiftly and helplessly, like a stone cast into the water, with no power to save themselves, straight to the bottom; the type the commentators draw, that in like manner shall mystical Babylon, the great enemy of the church, be destroyed and be found no more at all, where Babylon is cast down as a millstone into the sea; that God's judgments on his enemies are sudden, total, and irrecoverable).

themes: The depths have covered them
they sank into the bottom as a stone
the depths covering the Egyptians, who sank swiftly and helplessly as a stone
a type that mystical Babylon shall be cast down as a millstone, God's judgments sudden and total

schools.summary: Gill: the right hand of the Lord became glorious in bringing Israel out of Egypt and through the Red sea, and in the destruction of Pharaoh and the Egyptians; the witnesses note the song extolling the LORD's right hand as glorious in power, which had dashed the enemy in pieces — the praise of God's mighty power displayed in the deliverance and the destruction (the song magnifying the hand that wrought it: 'Thy right hand, O LORD, is become glorious in power: thy right hand, O LORD, hath dashed in pieces the enemy'; the figure, 'Thy right hand, O LORD,' the emblem of God's power put forth in action, here become glorious in power, winning glory by its mighty acts; the double work of the same hand, glorious both in saving Israel, bringing them out and through the sea, and in destroying the foe; the force of 'hath dashed in pieces the enemy,' that God's hand had utterly shattered and broken the proud enemy, as a vessel dashed to pieces; the repetition, 'Thy right hand, O LORD... thy right hand, O LORD,' the redoubled praise of the same almighty hand; that all the glory of the deliverance is given to God's power, not to man).

themes: Thy right hand, O LORD, is become glorious in power
thy right hand, O LORD, hath dashed in pieces the enemy
the LORD's right hand glorious, both saving Israel and shattering the foe
all the glory of the deliverance given to God's power, not to man

schools.summary: Gill: Christ has an excellency in him, a greatness of excellency, a superlative one, a more excellent name and nature than the angels, being a divine Person; the witnesses note the song extolling God's great excellency, by which he overthrew his enemies and sent forth wrath that consumed them as stubble — the praise of God's surpassing greatness in overthrowing the proud (the song magnifying the majesty that crushed the foe: 'in the greatness of thine excellency thou hast overthrown them that rose up against thee: thou sentest forth thy wrath, which consumed them as stubble'; the 'greatness of thine excellency,' God's surpassing majesty and glory, which Gill applies to Christ, who has a greatness of excellency, a superlative one, a more excellent name and nature than the angels; the overthrow, 'thou hast overthrown them that rose up against thee,' that those who proudly rose against God, for in fighting Israel they fought God, were cast down; the figure of the wrath, 'thou sentest forth thy wrath, which consumed them as stubble,' that God's anger, like a fire, devoured the enemy as easily and utterly as fire burns dry stubble; the folly and ruin of rising against God, whose excellency overthrows and whose wrath consumes).

themes: in the greatness of thine excellency thou hast overthrown them that rose up against thee
thou sentest forth thy wrath, which consumed them as stubble
God's surpassing excellency overthrowing those who rose against him
his wrath consuming them as fire burns stubble, the ruin of rising against God

schools.summary: Gill: the waters were gathered together and divided by a strong east wind, called the blast of the nostrils of the Lord, as easily brought by him as a man's breath; the witnesses note the song telling how, at the blast of God's nostrils, the waters were gathered, the floods stood upright as a heap, and the depths congealed — the marvel of the divided sea, the waters made to stand like walls at God's breath (the song recounting the dividing of the deep: 'with the blast of thy nostrils the waters were gathered together, the floods stood upright as an heap, and the depths were congealed in the heart of the sea'; the figure, 'the blast of thy nostrils,' God's mighty power, working by a strong east wind, spoken of as the breath of his nostrils, the dividing of the sea as easy to him as a man's breath; the wonder, 'the floods stood upright as an heap, and the depths were congealed,' that the waters, against all nature, stood up like walls or a heap, the very depths made firm as if frozen, that Israel might pass through dry-shod; that God can make the most yielding element stand firm at his word, and the most dreadful obey him; the power of God over the sea, his servant).

themes: with the blast of thy nostrils the waters were gathered together
the floods stood upright as an heap, and the depths were congealed in the heart of the sea
the waters standing upright as a heap at the blast of God's nostrils
God making the most yielding element stand firm at his word, the sea his servant

schools.summary: Gill: the enemy, that is, Pharaoh, who repented that he had let Israel go, an emblem of Satan, who when the people of God are taken out of his hands is uneasy and seeks to recover them, or of antichrist breathing out threatening and slaughter to the saints; the witnesses note the song rehearsing the enemy's boast, to pursue, overtake, divide the spoil, satisfy his lust, draw the sword, and destroy — the proud and bloody confidence of the foe, soon turned to ruin (the song mocking the vain boasting of Pharaoh: 'The enemy said, I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil... I will draw my sword, my hand shall destroy them'; the eager, hurried boast, the short broken clauses, 'I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil,' that Pharaoh, in his greedy and cruel confidence, counted the victory already his, dividing the spoil before he had it; the cruelty, 'my lust shall be satisfied upon them; I will draw my sword, my hand shall destroy them,' his fierce desire to glut his vengeance on the helpless people; the type the commentators draw, of Satan and antichrist, who, when the people of God are taken out of his hands, is uneasy and seeks to recover them, breathing out threatening and slaughter; the folly of the wicked, who boast of a victory God will turn to their ruin).

themes: The enemy said, I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil
I will draw my sword, my hand shall destroy them
the enemy's proud, greedy boast, counting the victory already his
a type of Satan seeking to recover God's people, the folly of the wicked who boast of a victory God turns to ruin

schools.summary: Gill: the strong east wind is the Lord Christ's, who has it in his treasury, holds it in his fists, sends it out as he pleases, and it fulfils his word; the witnesses note the song saying that God blew with his wind, the sea covered the enemy, and they sank as lead in the mighty waters — the sudden overthrow of the boaster by a breath of God (the proud foe sunk by a mere wind: 'Thou didst blow with thy wind, the sea covered them: they sank as lead in the mighty waters'; the contrast the song makes, that against all the enemy's boasting God needed but to blow with thy wind and the sea returned and covered them; that the wind was God's own, which he has in his treasury, holds in his fists, sends out as he pleases, fulfilling his word; the figure of their sinking, 'they sank as lead in the mighty waters,' that, weighed down by their armour and chariots, the proud host went down like lead, heavy and helpless, in the deep; the swift reversal, the boaster's 'I will destroy' answered by God's single breath that destroyed them; that God overthrows the proudest enemy with the least of his means).

themes: Thou didst blow with thy wind, the sea covered them
they sank as lead in the mighty waters
God needing but to blow with his wind for the sea to cover the boaster
the boaster's 'I will destroy' answered by God's single breath, overthrowing the proudest with the least of his means

schools.summary: Gill: none is like him among the mighty for the perfections of his nature, the blessings of his goodness, the works of his hands, and especially the greatness of his power in the salvation of his people and the ruin of their enemies; the witnesses note the song asking who is like the LORD among the gods, glorious in holiness, fearful in praises, doing wonders — the great confession of God's incomparable majesty and holiness (the redeemed extolling the matchless God: 'Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods? who is like thee, glorious in holiness, fearful in praises, doing wonders?'; the challenge, 'Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?' that none of the gods of the heathen, nor any of the mighty, can be compared to the LORD, in his nature, goodness, works, and power; the threefold praise, 'glorious in holiness,' his holiness the crown of his glory in which he is most to be admired, 'fearful in praises,' to be praised with reverence and holy fear, his praises themselves awful, and 'doing wonders,' the worker of marvels such as none else can do; that the deliverance at the sea proved God's incomparable greatness, above all the gods of Egypt and the world; that this is the abiding wonder of the redeemed, the matchlessness of their God).

debates.question: What does 'Who is like thee, glorious in holiness?' confess of God?

sides.position: God's incomparable majesty and holiness — none of the gods of the heathen nor any of the mighty can be compared to the LORD; his holiness the crown of his glory, to be praised with reverence and holy fear, the worker of wonders none else can do

themes: Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?
who is like thee, glorious in holiness, fearful in praises, doing wonders?
the great confession of God's incomparable majesty and holiness
his holiness the crown of his glory, to be praised with reverence and holy fear

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: thou stretchedst out thy right hand, the earth swallowed them; Gill: God exerted his power and gave a display of it, of which the right hand is an emblem; the witnesses note the song saying that God stretched out his right hand and the earth swallowed the enemy — the closing word of the first part, the foe swallowed up at the stretching of God's hand (the song marking the end of the enemy: 'Thou stretchedst out thy right hand, the earth swallowed them'; the act, 'Thou stretchedst out thy right hand,' God's power exerted and openly displayed, the right hand its emblem; the figure, 'the earth swallowed them,' that the enemy was swallowed up, whether by the sea, the watery deep, the lower parts of the earth, closing over them, or as the earth opened to receive the dead; that the proud host, that had boasted to destroy, was itself swallowed up and gone; the contrast, that the same hand that saved Israel destroyed the foe, the one act of power working both; that God's stretched-out hand is the death of his enemies and the salvation of his people).

themes: Thou stretchedst out thy right hand
the earth swallowed them
the foe swallowed up at the stretching of God's hand
the same hand saving Israel and destroying the foe, the death of his enemies and the salvation of his people

schools.summary: Gill: God led forth the people which he had redeemed from their bondage in Egypt, his peculiar people, guiding them through the Red sea toward Canaan; the witnesses note the song praising God who in mercy led forth his redeemed people and guided them by his strength to his holy habitation — the turn from the overthrow of the foe to the gracious leading of the redeemed (the song now singing God's guidance of his own: 'Thou in thy mercy hast led forth the people which thou hast redeemed: thou hast guided them in thy strength unto thy holy habitation'; the ground, 'in thy mercy,' that it was of God's free mercy, not their desert, that he led them forth; the people described, 'which thou hast redeemed,' that they were a redeemed people, God's own peculiar portion, bought out of bondage; the guidance, 'thou hast guided them in thy strength,' that the same strength that destroyed the foe now led and upheld his people on their way; the goal, 'unto thy holy habitation,' whether Canaan, the holy land, or Zion and the sanctuary, or, as the type reaches, the heavenly rest, the place of God's dwelling to which he brings his redeemed; that God leads his redeemed all the way to himself).

themes: Thou in thy mercy hast led forth the people which thou hast redeemed
thou hast guided them in thy strength unto thy holy habitation
God in free mercy leading and guiding his redeemed people
the same strength that destroyed the foe upholding his own, leading them all the way to himself

schools.summary: Gill: what follows from hence to the end of the song is plainly prophetic, a prediction of future events, the nations hearing the report of what God did to Egypt and being afraid; the witnesses note the song foretelling that the nations should hear and be afraid, sorrow taking hold on the inhabitants of Palestina — the prophetic foresight of the terror that should fall on the nations (the song turning prophet, foreseeing the fear of the peoples: 'The people shall hear, and be afraid: sorrow shall take hold on the inhabitants of Palestina'; the prophetic turn, that what follows to the end of the song is plainly prophetic, a prediction of how the nations should be affected; the cause of their fear, 'the people shall hear,' that the report of God's mighty acts on Egypt and at the sea should spread to all the nations, filling them with dread of so great a God and his people; the particular, 'sorrow shall take hold on the inhabitants of Palestina,' the Philistines, that they should be seized with anguish and pain at the news; that God's wonders for his people strike terror into their enemies, going before them to prepare their way; the comfort, that the fear of the nations was itself part of God's clearing the road to Canaan).

themes: The people shall hear, and be afraid
sorrow shall take hold on the inhabitants of Palestina
the song turning prophet, foreseeing the nations' terror at God's acts
God's wonders striking terror into the enemies, going before to prepare Israel's way

schools.summary: Gill: the dukes of Edom, of whom there were many, the land being governed by dukes, when they heard of the wonderful things done for Israel should be amazed; the witnesses note the song foretelling that the dukes of Edom should be amazed, the mighty men of Moab tremble, and all Canaan melt away — the prophecy of the dismay of the nations on Israel's road to Canaan (the song naming the peoples that should be struck with fear: 'the dukes of Edom shall be amazed; the mighty men of Moab, trembling shall take hold upon them; all the inhabitants of Canaan shall melt away'; the three peoples named, 'the dukes of Edom,' the chiefs of Esau's land, 'the mighty men of Moab,' and 'all the inhabitants of Canaan,' those through or near whose lands Israel should pass; the three degrees of their fear, 'amazed... trembling... melt away,' the dukes of Edom astonished, the mighty of Moab seized with trembling, and the Canaanites' hearts melting like water; the fulfilment the commentators trace, that this fear did indeed fall on these nations, as Rahab confessed, their hearts failing them at the report of God's wonders; that God so terrified the nations that even the mighty trembled before his weak people).

themes: the dukes of Edom shall be amazed; the mighty men of Moab, trembling shall take hold upon them
all the inhabitants of Canaan shall melt away
the prophecy of the nations' dismay, three degrees of fear
fulfilled as their hearts failed at God's wonders, God terrifying the mighty before his weak people

schools.summary: Gill: fear and dread should fall especially on the Canaanites, lest the Israelites should fall upon them and destroy them, or God should fight for them; the witnesses note the song foretelling that fear and dread should fall on the nations, who by the greatness of God's arm should be still as a stone, till his purchased people passed over — the prophecy of the nations held still by terror while Israel passed safely through (God's mighty arm freezing the foe with fear: 'Fear and dread shall fall upon them; by the greatness of thine arm they shall be as still as a stone; till thy people pass over, O LORD... which thou hast purchased'; the terror, 'Fear and dread shall fall upon them,' that the nations, especially the Canaanites, should be seized with such dread of Israel and Israel's God that they durst not stir; the figure, 'by the greatness of thine arm they shall be as still as a stone,' that God's mighty power should hold them motionless, struck stiff with fear, unable to oppose his passing people; the purpose, 'till thy people pass over... which thou hast purchased,' that the nations should be so held until Israel, God's purchased people, bought by the blood of the lamb and by the great redemption, passed safely through to their inheritance; the comfort, that God can hold the mightiest enemies still as stones while he brings his own safely home).

themes: Fear and dread shall fall upon them; by the greatness of thine arm they shall be as still as a stone
till thy people pass over, O LORD... which thou hast purchased
God's mighty arm freezing the nations with fear while his purchased people passed safely
God holding the mightiest enemies still as stones while he brings his own safely home

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: thou shalt bring them in, and plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance, in the place which thou hast made for thee to dwell in, the Sanctuary which thy hands have established; the witnesses note the song foretelling that God should bring his people in and plant them in the mountain of his inheritance, the place of his dwelling, the sanctuary his hands established — the prophecy of Israel settled in the holy land, where God should dwell among them (the song looking on to the settled rest and worship: 'Thou shalt bring them in, and plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance, in the place... which thou hast made for thee to dwell in, in the Sanctuary'; the bringing in, 'Thou shalt bring them in,' that God who brought them out of Egypt should also bring them into Canaan, both his work; the planting, 'plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance,' that they should be firmly settled, planted like a vine, in the land, especially Zion the mountain, which was God's inheritance; the great end, 'in the place... which thou hast made for thee to dwell in, in the Sanctuary,' that God's purpose was to dwell among his people in his sanctuary, the place of his abode and worship, established by his own hands; the type the commentators draw, of the church planted in God's holy hill, and of the heavenly sanctuary, the eternal dwelling of God with his redeemed).

themes: Thou shalt bring them in, and plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance
in the Sanctuary, O Lord, which thy hands have established
God bringing his people in and planting them where he should dwell among them
a type of the church planted in God's holy hill, and of the heavenly sanctuary

schools.summary: Gill: that same Lord spoken of throughout the song, to whom everything in it is ascribed, is no other than the Lord Jesus Christ, whose reign began in eternity and shall have no end; the witnesses note the song ending in the triumphant confession that the LORD shall reign for ever and ever — the crowning truth of the song, the everlasting kingdom of God (the song mounting at last to the eternal throne: 'The LORD shall reign for ever and ever'; the climax, that, having sung of God's victory, leading, and dwelling, the song crowns all with his reign for ever and ever, the unending kingship of the LORD; the Christological reading Gill gives, that this same LORD, to whom everything in it is ascribed, is no other than the Lord Jesus Christ, whose reign began in eternity and shall never end; the comfort to the redeemed, that their God and King, who had so gloriously delivered them, reigns for ever, and his kingdom cannot be shaken; that this is the great hope of the church, that the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord and of his Christ and he shall reign for ever and ever; that all God's works for his people flow from and lead to his eternal reign).

themes: The LORD shall reign
for ever and ever
the song crowning all with the everlasting kingdom of God
the comfort that their God and King reigns for ever, his kingdom unshakable, the great hope of the church

schools.summary: Gill: the horse of Pharaoh, all his horses and horsemen, went into the sea, and the Lord brought the waters again upon them, while Israel went on dry land; the witnesses note the recalling of how Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen went into the sea and the LORD brought the waters back upon them, while Israel passed on dry land — the great contrast that grounds the song, the foe drowned and the people saved (the verse summing the reason of all the praise: 'the horse of Pharaoh went in... into the sea, and the LORD brought again the waters of the sea upon them; but the children of Israel went on dry land'; the destruction of the enemy, 'the LORD brought again the waters of the sea upon them,' that the same waters which had stood up as walls returned and drowned Pharaoh's whole host; the salvation of the people, 'but the children of Israel went on dry land in the midst of the sea,' that the very path that destroyed the Egyptians was a safe, dry road for Israel; the great distinction, the one act of God meaning death to the foe and life to his people; that this is the sum and reason of the whole song, the just ground of all their praise).

themes: the horse of Pharaoh went in... into the sea, and the LORD brought again the waters of the sea upon them
but the children of Israel went on dry land in the midst of the sea
the great contrast grounding the song, the foe drowned and the people saved
the one act of God meaning death to the foe and life to his people

schools.summary: Gill: Miriam, very probably the same called the sister of Moses, her name signifying bitterness, having it from the bitterness of the times, took a timbrel and led the women; the witnesses note Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, taking a timbrel, and all the women going out after her with timbrels and dances — the women joining the song of triumph, led by Miriam (the women too breaking forth in praise: 'Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a timbrel in her hand; and all the women went out after her with timbrels and with dances'; the title, 'Miriam the prophetess,' that she was honoured as a prophetess and the sister of Aaron and Moses, one of the leaders God set over his people; the manner, 'took a timbrel in her hand... with timbrels and with dances,' that she led the women in a joyful, festal celebration, with instruments and dancing, as was the custom in great triumphs; the name Miriam, signifying bitterness, from the bitter times of Israel's bondage, now turned to joy; that the whole congregation, men and women, joined in praising God for the deliverance; that great mercies call for full and public thanksgiving).

themes: Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a timbrel in her hand
and all the women went out after her with timbrels and with dances
Miriam the prophetess leading the women in joyful, festal praise
the whole congregation joining, great mercies calling for full and public thanksgiving

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Miriam answered them, that is, responded, leading the women in singing the refrain antiphonally to the men; Gill notes she repeated the same song, of which the first clause is given; the witnesses note Miriam answering with the song, 'Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously,' repeating the opening words — the antiphonal response of the women, taking up the song's refrain (Miriam leading the answering chorus: 'Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously; the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea'; the manner, 'Miriam answered them,' that the women answered or responded to the men, singing the same words back to them, the song raised antiphonally, in alternate parts; that Miriam led the women in the refrain, 'Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously,' the very words with which the song began, the heart of the praise; that thus the whole congregation, men and women answering one another, joined in one great song of triumph; the fitness of such responsive praise, all voices uniting in the LORD's glory; that the deliverance was celebrated with full and ordered joy).

themes: Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously
the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea
Miriam leading the women in the answering refrain, antiphonally
men and women answering one another in one great song of triumph

schools.summary: Gill: Moses brought Israel from the Red sea, or caused them to journey, some think with difficulty, they being intent on the spoil cast up on the shore; the witnesses note Moses bringing Israel from the Red Sea into the wilderness of Shur, where they went three days and found no water — the swift turn from triumph to trial, the want of water in the desert (the song of joy soon followed by a hard trial: 'Moses brought Israel from the Red sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur; and they went three days in the wilderness, and found no water'; the move, 'Moses brought Israel from the Red sea,' some say with difficulty, the people lingering over the spoil washed up on the shore, leading them on into the wilderness of Shur; the trial, 'they went three days in the wilderness, and found no water,' that, so soon after the great deliverance and the song of praise, they came into a dry place and for three days found no water, a sore want in the desert; the commentators' note that God often leads his people from joy to trial, that their faith may be exercised; that the same people who had just sung God's praise were quickly brought to a hard test).

themes: So Moses brought Israel from the Red sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur
and they went three days in the wilderness, and found no water
the swift turn from triumph to trial, three days without water
God leading his people from joy to trial, that their faith may be exercised

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: when they came to Marah, they could not drink of the waters of Marah, for they were bitter, and so the place was named Marah; Gill notes the place was so called by anticipation from the quality of the waters; the witnesses note Israel coming to Marah and finding the waters too bitter to drink, so that the place was named Marah — the bitter disappointment after the long thirst, the waters they found unfit to drink (the hoped-for relief proving a fresh grief: 'when they came to Marah, they could not drink of the waters of Marah, for they were bitter: therefore the name of it was called Marah'; the hope and the let-down, that after three days' thirst they came at last to water but could not drink of the waters of Marah, for they were bitter, the longed-for water found undrinkable, a sharper grief than no water at all; the naming, 'the name of it was called Marah,' which means bitterness, from the bitter quality of the waters; that God's people often find their expected comforts turned to bitterness, that they may learn not to rest in the creature; that this bitter trial was the occasion of a great mercy and a great lesson to follow).

themes: when they came to Marah, they could not drink of the waters of Marah, for they were bitter
therefore the name of it was called Marah
the bitter disappointment, the longed-for water found too bitter to drink
God's people often finding their expected comforts turned to bitterness, that they learn not to rest in the creature

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the people were in a state of great mental degradation, owing to their long and oppressive bondage, and so were quick to murmur; the witnesses note the people murmuring against Moses, asking what they should drink — the quick rising of unbelief and discontent at the first trial (the people, so lately singing, now murmuring: 'the people murmured against Moses, saying, What shall we drink?'; the sin, 'the people murmured against Moses,' that, instead of crying to God or trusting him who had so lately delivered them, they fell to murmuring and complaining against Moses, and so against God who sent him; the cause Clarke marks, the great mental degradation of a people owing to their long and oppressive bondage, quick to despair and complain; the shame of it, that those who had just sung the song of triumph so soon forgot God's power and goodness at the first want; the lesson, that unbelief is quick to murmur, and that yesterday's mercies are soon forgotten in today's trial; that murmuring against God's ministers is murmuring against God).

themes: And the people murmured against Moses
saying, What shall we drink?
the people, so lately singing, now murmuring at the first trial
unbelief quick to complain, yesterday's mercies soon forgotten, murmuring against God's minister is against God

schools.summary: Gill: Moses cried unto the Lord, or prayed; happy it is to have a God to go to in time of trouble, whose hand is not shortened; the witnesses note Moses crying to the LORD, who showed him a tree to cast into the waters to make them sweet, and there made a statute and proved them — the prayer answered by the tree that healed the bitter waters, a type of the cross (the bitter made sweet at God's word: 'he cried unto the LORD; and the LORD shewed him a tree, which when he had cast into the waters, the waters were made sweet... and there he proved them'; the resort to God, 'he cried unto the LORD,' that Moses, unlike the murmuring people, prayed, the right course in trouble, happy to have a God to go to; the remedy, 'the LORD shewed him a tree, which... cast into the waters, the waters were made sweet,' that God provided a tree whose casting in healed the bitterness, a thing beyond the tree's own virtue, God working by it; the rich type the commentators draw, of the cross of Christ, or Christ himself the tree of life, cast into the bitter waters of affliction and of a sinful life, sweetening all to the believer; the statute, 'there he made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there he proved them,' that God gave them a law and put them to the test, to try their obedience).

debates.question: What does the tree that sweetened the bitter waters of Marah typify?

sides.position: The cross of Christ (or Christ himself, the tree of life) — cast into the bitter waters of affliction and of a sinful life, it sweetens all to the believer; the bitter made sweet at God's word, beyond the tree's own virtue

themes: he cried unto the LORD; and the LORD shewed him a tree... the waters were made sweet
there he made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there he proved them
Moses' prayer answered by the tree that healed the bitter waters
a type of the cross of Christ, sweetening the bitter waters of affliction to the believer

schools.summary: Gill: by this and the following words they are prepared to expect a body of laws, the rule of their future conduct, with a promise of preservation from the diseases of Egypt; the witnesses note God promising that, if they would hearken to his voice and keep his statutes, he would put none of Egypt's diseases on them, for he is the LORD that healeth them — the covenant of obedience and the gracious name Jehovah-Rapha (God binding health to obedience and revealing himself as Healer: 'If thou wilt diligently hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God... I will put none of these diseases upon thee... for I am the LORD that healeth thee'; the condition, 'If thou wilt diligently hearken... and keep all his statutes,' that God set before them obedience to his voice and law as the way of blessing; the promise, 'I will put none of these diseases upon thee, which I have brought upon the Egyptians,' that, obeying, they should be spared the plagues and sicknesses that fell on Egypt, kept in health; the gracious name revealed, 'for I am the LORD that healeth thee,' Jehovah-Rapha, that God is the healer of his people, both of body and, the commentators add, of the soul, in Christ the great Physician; that all true health and healing are of God, who both keeps from disease and heals it).

debates.question: What does the name 'I am the LORD that healeth thee' (Jehovah-Rapha) reveal?

sides.position: God as the healer of his people — both of body, sparing the obedient Egypt's diseases, and (the commentators add) of the soul, in Christ the great Physician; all true health and healing are of God, who both keeps from disease and heals it

themes: If thou wilt diligently hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God... I will put none of these diseases upon thee
for I am the LORD that healeth thee
God binding health to obedience and revealing himself as Jehovah-Rapha
the healer of his people, of body and of soul in Christ the great Physician

schools.summary: Gill: they came to Elim on the twenty-fifth of Nisan, staying but one day at Marah; Elim had twelve wells of water and seventy palm trees; the witnesses note Israel coming to Elim, where were twelve wells of water and seventy palm trees, and encamping there by the waters — the gracious refreshment after the trial, the abundance of water and shade (God leading them from the bitter to the sweet, from Marah to Elim: 'they came to Elim, where were twelve wells of water, and threescore and ten palm trees: and they encamped there by the waters'; the contrast with Marah, that from the bitter, scant waters they came to twelve wells of water, abundance and to spare, and threescore and ten palm trees for shade and fruit, a place of rest and plenty; the providence, that God, having tried them at Marah, now refreshed them at Elim, tempering the trial with comfort; the fancy some make of the numbers, the twelve wells answering to the twelve tribes and the seventy palms to the seventy elders or the seventy souls of Jacob's house, though this is but a pleasant observation; the comfort, that God does not try his people beyond measure but brings them to refreshing after the bitter waters).

themes: And they came to Elim, where were twelve wells of water, and threescore and ten palm trees
and they encamped there by the waters
the gracious refreshment at Elim after the bitter trial of Marah
God tempering the trial with comfort, not trying his people beyond measure
